partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for icelake[¶](#default-submission-script-for-icelake "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI and requiring no more than 3370 MiB per cpu[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi-and-requiring-no-more-than-3370-mib-per-cpu "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the icelake partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
icelake partition.

### Jobs requiring more than 3370 MiB per cpu[¶](#jobs-requiring-more-than-3370-mib-per-cpu "Permalink to this headline")

In the case of larger memory requirements, it is most efficient to submit
instead to the icelake-himem partition which will allocate 6760 MiB per cpu:

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

If this amount of memory per cpu is insufficient you will need to
specify either the *–mem=* or *–cpus-per-task=* directive, in
addition to the *-p* directive, in order to make sure you have enough
memory at run time. Note that in this case, Slurm will satisfy the
memory requirement by allocating (and charging for) more cpus

benchmark_annotation.summary: Unusually long queue time

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Scheduling / Queue Wait Time
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that a short GPU job has been waiting much longer than usual. This does not by itself indicate an account or script fault; queue time can increase when demand is high, and queued jobs gain priority over time.

Cancelling and resubmitting is counterproductive because it removes the job's accumulated queue priority.

ACTION

1) Reassure the user that long waits can happen when demand is high, even for jobs that usually start quickly.
2) Advise the user not to cancel and resubmit queued jobs unless necessary.
3) If they want a rough estimate, suggest:
   `squeue -u <CRSid> --start`
4) Clarify that starting an interactive job does not change the priority of an already queued batch job.
5) Escalate only if there is evidence of scheduler malfunction rather than normal contention.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Kai,

From the information provided, there does not appear to be anything obviously wrong with your job submission. Queue times can change significantly depending on current demand, so a job that usually starts quickly may occasionally wait much longer.

Please avoid cancelling and resubmitting the job unless necessary, as that resets its place in the queue and removes any priority it has accumulated while waiting.

If you want a rough estimate of when a queued job may start, you can run:

  squeue -u <your CRSid> --start

Also, starting an interactive job will not alter the priority of an existing queued batch job.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not promise a fixed start time.
- Do not imply scheduler fault without evidence.
- Warn users that cancelling/resubmitting resets queue age.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Scheduling / Queue Time
Technical Action Required: No
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user's job has been in the queue for an unusually long time (15 hours) for a GPU job that typically takes around an hour to start [2][4][6]. The job is requesting 1 node with 1 GPU for 20 minutes, which is a relatively short job [5]. There is no indication of any issues with the user's account or job submission script [2][4].

ACTION
Please be patient and do not cancel and resubmit the job, as this can be counterproductive [2][4][6]. The job will run when resources become available.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide more details about your job submission script, including the wall clock time setting?
- Are there any other jobs in the queue that are taking priority over yours?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your patience. There is nothing wrong with your account or jobs, except that they are likely requesting resources that are not yet available [2]. Please bear in mind that each paying job can occupy its resources for 36 hours [2]. Your jobs will run when resources become available.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not cancel and resubmit jobs unnecessarily, as this can decrease their priority [2][4][6].
- Be aware that paying jobs have higher priority than non-paying jobs [2][6].

REFERENCE KEY
[2] : HPCSSUP-59843
[4] : HPCSSUP-81233
[5] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/batch.html::chunk::0005
[6] : HPCSSUP-59877

Also, starting an interactive job will not alter the priority of an existing queued batch job.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not promise a fixed start time.
- Do not imply scheduler fault without evidence.
- Warn users that cancelling/resubmitting resets queue age.

partition directive, i.e.

```

```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

```

where 1 <= N <= 4.

Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA Ampere A100 GPUs, with 1000GB host RAM and two AMD EPYC 64-core processors.

Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 3 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.

### Submitting the job to the queuing system[¶](#submitting-the-job-to-the-queuing-system "Permalink to this headline")

The command sbatch is used to submit jobs, e.g.

```
sbatch submission_script
```

The command will return a unique job identifier, which is used to query and control the job and to identify output. See the man page (*man sbatch*) for more options.

The following more complex example submits a job array with index values between 1 and 7 with a step size of 2 (i.e. 1, 3, 5 and 7) to the project STARS-SL2-CPU:

```
sbatch --array=1-7:2 -A STARS-SL2-CPU submission_script
```

### Deleting jobs[¶](#deleting-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

To cancel a job (either running or still queuing) use scancel:

```
scancel <jobid>
```

The <jobid> is printed when the job is submitted, alternatively use the commands squeue, qstat or showq to obtain the job ID.

benchmark_annotation.summary: HPC password

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH Login / Password Clarification
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is attempting to SSH to login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk and reports not knowing their HPC password, stating they may never have set one.

On CSD3, there is no separate HPC-specific password for internal users. Authentication uses the user’s UIS/Raven password, followed by the appropriate CSD3 TOTP token for SSH access.

ACTION

1) Clarify authentication model
   - HPC password = UIS/Raven password.
   - There is no separate password to set during activation.

2) Clarify SSH login sequence
   When connecting via:
     ssh <CRSid>@login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk

   The user should:
     a) Enter their current UIS/Raven password.
     b) Enter their TOTP code associated with CSD3 SSH (typically labelled “CSD3:ssh” or similar in their authenticator app).

3) If login still fails
   Ask:
     - Have you recently changed your UIS/Raven password?
     - Do you have the correct CSD3 SSH MFA token configured?
     - What exact error message appears after entering password/TOTP?

4) Do NOT:
   - Reset password directly.
   - Provide or request passwords.
   - Create a separate HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC password

Dear Fay,

There is no separate HPC password to set up. Your HPC login password is the same as your UIS/Raven password.

When connecting via SSH, please enter your current UIS/Raven password when prompted, followed by the TOTP code associated with your CSD3 SSH login (usually labelled “CSD3:ssh” in your authenticator app).

If you continue to experience issues, please let us know the exact error message shown after entering your credentials.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Never request or store user passwords.
- Do not imply a separate HPC password exists.
- Escalate only if authentication failures persist after confirming correct credential usage.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account / Login Issue [2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [2]
Portal Action Required: No [2]
Urgency: Medium [2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble logging in to the HPC server and is unsure if they need to create a new password [2]. It appears that the user may not have set up their password correctly or may be using an incorrect password [4].

ACTION
To resolve the issue, the user should try using their UIS password, which is the same password used for Raven [4]. If the user is still having trouble, they can try resetting their password using the UIS Password Management Application [5]. Additionally, the user should ensure that they have set up their MFA token correctly, as this is required for login [8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried using your UIS password to log in?
- Have you set up your MFA token correctly?
- Are you receiving any error messages when attempting to log in?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your help. I will try using my UIS password and setting up my MFA token. If I am still having trouble, I will let you know.
Best regards,
Fay

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users should always use their UIS password to log in to the HPC server [4].
- MFA tokens are required for login and should be set up correctly [8].

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH Login / Password Clarification
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is attempting to SSH to login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk and reports not knowing their HPC password, stating they may never have set one.

On CSD3, there is no separate HPC-specific password for internal users. Authentication uses the user’s UIS/Raven password, followed by the appropriate CSD3 TOTP token for SSH access.

ACTION

1) Clarify authentication model
   - HPC password = UIS/Raven password.
   - There is no separate password to set during activation.

2) Clarify SSH login sequence
   When connecting via:
     ssh <CRSid>@login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk

   The user should:
     a) Enter their current UIS/Raven password.
     b) Enter their TOTP code associated with CSD3 SSH (typically labelled “CSD3:ssh” or similar in their authenticator app).

3) If login still fails
   Ask:
     - Have you recently changed your UIS/Raven password?
     - Do you have the correct CSD3 SSH MFA token configured?
     - What exact error message appears after entering password/TOTP?

4) Do NOT:
   - Reset password directly.
   - Provide or request passwords.
   - Create a separate HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC password

Dear Fay,

There is no separate HPC password to set up. Your HPC login password is the same as your UIS/Raven password.

When connecting via SSH, please enter your current UIS/Raven password when prompted, followed by the TOTP code associated with your CSD3 SSH login (usually labelled “CSD3:ssh” in your authenticator app).

If you continue to experience issues, please let us know the exact error message shown after entering your credentials.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Never request or store user passwords.
- Do not imply a separate HPC password exists.
- Escalate only if authentication failures persist after confirming correct credential usage.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble logging in to the HPC server and is unsure if they need to create a new password [2]. It appears that the user may not have set up their password correctly or may be using an incorrect password [4].

ACTION
To resolve the issue, the user should try using their UIS password, which is the same password used for Raven [4]. If the user is still having trouble, they can try resetting their password using the UIS Password Management Application [5]. Additionally, the user should ensure that they have set up their MFA token correctly, as this is required for login [8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried using your UIS password to log in?
- Have you set up your MFA token correctly?
- Are you receiving any error messages when attempting to log in?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your help. I will try using my UIS password and setting up my MFA token. If I am still having trouble, I will let you know.
Best regards,
Fay

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users should always use their UIS password to log in to the HPC server [4].
- MFA tokens are required for login and should be set up correctly [8].

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a

# PI Dawn Access[¶](#pi-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (IdP of last resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

[![Access ID Login Option](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)

If this is your first time accessing MyAccessID, please ensure that you provide the necessary details and take a moment to review their policy. If you have further issues with using MyAccessID and you have a university email address, please contact your local IT department to ensure you are

[TICKET_SUMMARY] HPC password

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC team,
I am trying to access my hpc account, which I usually accessed by `ssh hpc`, having everything configure in the .ssh/config file.
I am now being asked a Password, which unfortunately I don't remember.
Is there an easy solution to this.
Thank you and best wishes,
Mark

passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Note that the security of both users’ data and the service itself depends strongly on choosing the

# User Dawn Access[¶](#user-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## Users Accessing Portal[¶](#users-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (Identity of Last Resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-user.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

[![Access ID Login Option](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)

If this is your first time accessing MyAccessID, please ensure that you provide the necessary details and take a moment to review their policy. If you have further issues with using MyAccessID and you have a university email address, please contact your local IT department to

benchmark_annotation.summary: SRCP Epi platform - storage query

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Storage Architecture / RFS
Technical Action Required: No (clarification only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

FOCUS OF RESPONSE
User’s second query:

“My understanding is that somehow storage for SRCP is provisioned on RFS. Is this RFS dedicated hardware that is isolated for use by a particular platform? Or does it work differently? In which case, is there the option to have dedicated RCS hardware, which will be cheaper?”

QUICK ASSESSMENT
SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS-backed storage that is logically and operationally isolated per platform/project. It is not pooled with general-purpose RDS/RCS environments, and users cannot directly mount external RCS storage within SRCP. There is no model for attaching separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a cheaper alternative.

ACTION

1) Clarify storage model
   - SRCP storage is provisioned as isolated RFS shares associated specifically with SRCP projects.
   - These shares are separated from other storage systems and not user-configurable.
   - The isolation is by design due to SRCP security requirements.

2) Clarify hardware/cost question
   - SRCP storage is not dynamically attached from arbitrary RCS pools.
   - There is no option to provision separate “dedicated RCS hardware” for SRCP at a reduced cost.
   - SRCP environments have a defined architecture and approved storage pathways only.

3) If cost optimisation is the underlying concern
   - Suggest reviewing retention strategy (e.g., archive/export workflow outside SRCP if appropriate).
   - Explain that external storage (e.g., RCS) would require controlled export and re-import, not live mounting.

4) Do NOT:
   - Suggest bypassing SRCP isolation.
   - Imply that SRCP storage can be merged with other RCS/RDS resources.
   - Promise alternative hardware provisioning unless formally approved.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: SRCP storage architecture

Dear Tom,

SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS shares that are logically and operationally isolated for SRCP projects. These are separate from other RCS/RDS storage systems and are not directly accessible or mountable from outside the platform.

It is not possible to attach separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a lower-cost alternative. The SRCP architecture and its storage pathways are fixed by design due to the security requirements of the platform.

If you are looking to reduce storage costs, one option would be to export data from SRCP and archive it to a nominated RCS project outside the platform, but this would not be a live integration.

Please let us know if you would like to discuss possible workflows for archiving data.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Maintain SRCP security model integrity.
- Do not suggest storage sharing across security domains.
- Avoid speculative statements about hardware provisioning beyond documented architecture.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Archive
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
RFS and RCS are available storage options on CSD3 [2][6]. RFS is suitable for highly resilient group-storage with hourly backups [2], while RCS is designed for long-term storage of inactive, archive data [2][6]. 

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To archive old projects, you can purchase an RCS storage project via the self-service portal [2][6]. If you need help with transferring data from RFS to RCS, please provide the RFS project name and the directories you wish to transfer, and we can assist with the migration [5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the estimated size of the data you wish to archive?
- Do you have an existing RFS or RCS project, or would you like to set up a new one?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To archive your old projects, you can purchase an RCS storage project via the self-service portal: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/. If you need help with transferring data from RFS to RCS, please let us know and we can assist with the migration.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Data migration should be handled carefully to avoid data loss [5].
- Please ensure you have the necessary permissions and access rights before transferring data [5].

REFERENCE KEY
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/wbic.html::chunk::0004
[5] : HPCSSUP-44043
[6] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/wbic.html::chunk::0006

retrieval_trace.text:
[TICKET_SUMMARY] Request storage usage RCS

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
I would like to know how much storage space is left on our RCS archive-jan33 please. 
Thank you very much.

service is available on CSD3 through our [RFS service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-file-share),
which can now be provided as an *NFS-mountable dataset on CSD3* designed for
highly resilient group-storage with hourly backups.

RFS is sold via the [self-service gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk).

Note

RFS is usually sold as an SMB fileshare for users who wish to
mount the storage on their desktops/laptops across the Cambridge
University Network. However the above-mentioned NFS-share on CSD3 is
available on request. Please contact us at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to
discuss if this is of interest.

#### /lustre/archive[¶](#lustre-archive "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC had a special archive storage area on the /lustre filesystem from
which data was migrated off to a tape-based storage service.

CSD3 offers the [RCS storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-cold-store)
as an equivalent for this, providing a location for inactive, archive
data for long-term storage.

[Purchasing](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RCS/)
links are provided.

### PACS Access[¶](#pacs-access "Permalink to this headline")

Users using data from PACS will be able to continue to do so from the
CSD3 login nodes.

To do this you will need to have a CSD3 account already, and to
migrate the credentials used to authenticate you to the PACS server,
from your WBIC home-directory to CSD3.

This can be performed as follows:

```
user@wbic-gate-3:~$ dcm_migrate
```

This should only need to be performed once, and will require your
password to be entered. Your access to PACS

headline")

Warning

The WBIC platform contained a number of different storage areas for
users, all of which will be **retired** with the closure of the platform.

All data should be migrated to an alternative storage platform on
CSD3 or downloaded to another location before the platform is
shut-down.

Below we outline alternative services on CSD3 for each storage area that
are available for purchase so that you can replicate the WBIC
environment as best as possible.

#### /lustre[¶](#lustre "Permalink to this headline")

The WBIC dedicated Lustre filesystem (/lustre) is replaced by our [RDS
storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store)
which provides equivalent large-scale scratch storage.

All CSD3 users get a free 1TB personal scratch space, but all
group-shared capacity must be purchased through our [Storage self-service
gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RDS/)
Owners of group directories will be contacted separately to make arrangements.

#### /home[¶](#home "Permalink to this headline")

All WBIC users had 100GB of /home space, however on CSD3 this is reduced
to 40GB. Users with usage greater than 40GB will be required to either
reduce their usage under this amount, or to purchase additional RFS or
RDS storage and move excess data to these areas.

#### /data[¶](#data "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC users also had access to the /data storage location, which benefited
from the same resilient storage platform as /home, including hourly
snapshots and data replication, designed for datasets requiring durable
backups.

A similar

 is in the correct locations. The portal is mainly for book keeping and doesn't directly interact with file system, so we can intervene in cases like this.

Best,
Dominic

﻿On 08/04/2022, 16:09, "Dominic Friend" <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk> wrote:
—-—-—-—
Reply above this line.
Dominic Friend commented:
Hi Sarah,
This is definitely something we can assist with.
We can do a capacity analysis of the directories that you have listed so that we can quote you for a new RCS space. Once that is paid for and created we can facilitate the movement of the data on our end.
Once the copy is complete we can delete the copied data from RFS to allow for the shrinkage. At this point we'll know the remaining usage and we'll round it up to the nearest TB and +1TB as you request. And this is the number we'll shrink the RFS license to in the portal, so then you can renew for the correct cost using the portal.
If things are getting a little tight for time, we can invoke the shrink of RFS in the portal earlier, and delay reflecting the changes on the file-system until all the data is in the correct locations. The portal is mainly for book keeping and doesn't directly interact with file system, so we can intervene in cases like this.
Regarding your RCS lifecycle question can you clarify what you mean by lifecycle? Are you referring to our plans for the lifetime of RCS as a service?
Best,
Dominic

Eg: data under /lustre/group,
/lustre/archive or any group-owned data under other locations.

This data will require being moved to an RDS (scratch), RCS
(archive) or RFS (resilient, backup) project on CSD3.

Group data should be purchased as a group, which would typically be
done by the PI for your research group. Additionally, given the
quantities of data involved, we can perform the data migration for
your group so that it is carried out as efficiently as possible.

Important

Please encourage your PI to get in touch with us at
support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to discuss how we can assist the setup and
migration of group data to CSD3.

We will be attempting to contact all known PIs to assist in this
process, but being proactive here can help ensure that we can make
this transition as seamless as possible for your group.

#### 3. Purchase any required storage projects on CSD3[¶](#purchase-any-required-storage-projects-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Purchases can be made via our [storage portal](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk).
Projects greater than 100TB in size are eligible for a volume
pricing discount.

#### 4. Migrate individual files to CSD3[¶](#migrate-individual-files-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

For relatively modest quantities of data less than 1TB, you should be
able to migrate these yourself with `rsync`. An example command to
use would be,

```
user@wbic-gate-3:~$ rsync --verbose --info=progress2 --archive --no-o --no-g \
 --whole-file --update --inplace --rsh \
 "ssh -T -x -o Compression=no -c aes128-gcm@openssh.com" \
 ~/

# End of SBS allocation scheme[¶](#end-of-sbs-allocation-scheme "Permalink to this headline")

Important

The School of Biological Sciences ‘SBS’ resource allocation scheme ended on 31 Dec 2022.

Any PIs and users still wishing to retain their current storage projects should respond to the automated renewal notices sent from the self-service portal prior to 31 Dec. Or alternatively, contact the servicedesk at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance in identifying the relevant projects or migrating the data elsewhere.

## Key Deadlines[¶](#key-deadlines "Permalink to this headline")

### From 1 January 2023[¶](#from-1-january-2023 "Permalink to this headline")

* Any storage projects not yet renewed have been flagged as ‘suspended’ in the
  storage portal.
* Data owners and managers can still generate quotes and submit purchase orders to renew,
  which will be automatically backdated to be effective from 1 Jan.
* Access to data is still possible during the 30 day grace period for late renewals,
  after which access to the data will be lost.

### From 30 January 2023 (end of grace period)[¶](#from-30-january-2023-end-of-grace-period "Permalink to this headline")

* User access to unrenewed projects will be lost.
* All data in projects will **only be accessible on request**.
  Access will be provided in a read-only mode in cases where it is necessary to migrate
  data to an alternative location on CSD3 or offsite.

### From 1 July 2023[¶](#from-1-july-2023 "Permalink to this headline")

* In accordance with our [terms and

service is available on CSD3 through our [RFS service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-file-share),
which can now be provided as an *NFS-mountable dataset on CSD3* designed for
highly resilient group-storage with hourly backups.

RFS is sold via the [self-service gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk).

Note

RFS is usually sold as an SMB fileshare for users who wish to
mount the storage on their desktops/laptops across the Cambridge
University Network. However the above-mentioned NFS-share on CSD3 is
available on request. Please contact us at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to
discuss if this is of interest.

#### /lustre/archive[¶](#lustre-archive "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC had a special archive storage area on the /lustre filesystem from
which data was migrated off to a tape-based storage service.

CSD3 offers the [RCS storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-cold-store)
as an equivalent for this, providing a location for inactive, archive
data for long-term storage.

[Purchasing](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RCS/)
links are provided.

### PACS Access[¶](#pacs-access "Permalink to this headline")

Users using data from PACS will be able to continue to do so from the
CSD3 login nodes.

To do this you will need to have a CSD3 account already, and to
migrate the credentials used to authenticate you to the PACS server,
from your WBIC home-directory to CSD3.

This can be performed as follows:

```
user@wbic-gate-3:~$ dcm_migrate
```

This should only need to be performed once, and will require your
password to be entered. Your access to PACS

headline")

Warning

The WBIC platform contained a number of different storage areas for
users, all of which will be **retired** with the closure of the platform.

All data should be migrated to an alternative storage platform on
CSD3 or downloaded to another location before the platform is
shut-down.

Below we outline alternative services on CSD3 for each storage area that
are available for purchase so that you can replicate the WBIC
environment as best as possible.

#### /lustre[¶](#lustre "Permalink to this headline")

The WBIC dedicated Lustre filesystem (/lustre) is replaced by our [RDS
storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store)
which provides equivalent large-scale scratch storage.

All CSD3 users get a free 1TB personal scratch space, but all
group-shared capacity must be purchased through our [Storage self-service
gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RDS/)
Owners of group directories will be contacted separately to make arrangements.

#### /home[¶](#home "Permalink to this headline")

All WBIC users had 100GB of /home space, however on CSD3 this is reduced
to 40GB. Users with usage greater than 40GB will be required to either
reduce their usage under this amount, or to purchase additional RFS or
RDS storage and move excess data to these areas.

#### /data[¶](#data "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC users also had access to the /data storage location, which benefited
from the same resilient storage platform as /home, including hourly
snapshots and data replication, designed for datasets requiring durable
backups.

A similar

Eg: data under /lustre/group,

This data will require being moved to an RDS (scratch), RCS

your group so that it is carried out as efficiently as possible.

this transition as seamless as possible for your group.

#### 3. Purchase any required storage projects on CSD3[¶](#purchase-any-required-storage-projects-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Purchases can be made via our [storage portal](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk).
Projects greater than 100TB in size are eligible for a volume
pricing discount.

#### 4. Migrate individual files to CSD3[¶](#migrate-individual-files-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

For relatively modest quantities of data less than 1TB, you should be
able to migrate these yourself with `rsync`. An example command to
use would be,

```
user@wbic-gate-3:~$ rsync --verbose --info=progress2 --archive --no-o --no-g \
 --whole-file --update --inplace --rsh \
 "ssh -T -x -o Compression=no -c aes128-gcm@openssh.com" \
 ~/

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS & RFS / Provisioning
Technical Action Required: No (user action pending)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (access pending)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
A new user (vj292) has been added to both an RDS and RFS-NFS project via the self-service portal but remains in “Provisioning” state. The most common cause of this status is that the user has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions in the UIS Self Service portal. Until this step is completed, the provisioning workflow cannot proceed and access will not be activated.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm provisioning state (internal)
   - Verify that the user appears as “Provisioning” on the relevant RDS and RFS projects.
   - Check whether Terms & Conditions acceptance is outstanding.

2) Instruct user to accept Terms & Conditions
   Ask the new member (vj292) to:
   - Visit: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/
   - Click on each relevant storage project:
       • rds-djh1002-hodson-rds
       • rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs
   - Accept the storage Terms & Conditions for each project.

3) Explain workflow
   - Once Terms & Conditions are accepted, a provisioning request is automatically generated.
   - HPC Support will then activate access.
   - All relevant parties will receive automatic email confirmation once provisioning completes.

4) Do NOT:
   - Manually override provisioning before Terms & Conditions are accepted.
   - Escalate unless provisioning remains stalled after acceptance.

IF PROVISIONING DOES NOT COMPLETE AFTER ACCEPTANCE

- Confirm acceptance timestamp in portal.
- Check whether an automatic provisioning ticket was generated.
- Escalate to storage administration only if backend provisioning fails after acceptance.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Provisioning status for new lab member

Dear Joanna,

Thank you for your message.

Victor’s access is currently showing as “Provisioning” because he has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions.

Could he please visit:

  https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/

and click on the relevant project links to accept the Terms & Conditions for each storage project?

Once this is completed, a provisioning request will automatically be generated and we will activate his access. All parties will receive confirmation by email once this has been processed.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not bypass the mandatory Terms & Conditions acceptance process.
- Do not grant access before provisioning workflow completes.
- Ensure both RDS and RFS projects are covered if both were requested.

Dear HPC support team, 
Apologies for bothering you about this.
A couple of days ago, I added Victor (CC’d; CRSid: vj292) to our lab RDS (rds-djh1002-hodson-rds) and RFS-NFS (rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs) space. However, in the self-service portal he is still showing as “Provisioning”.
I just wanted to check whether there is anything we can do to speed up access to the data, or whether there is something we may have missed on our end.
Many thanks for your help.
Best wishes, 
Joanna

Dear HPC support team, 
Apologies for bothering you about this.
A couple of days ago, I added Victor (CC’d; CRSid: vj292) to our lab RDS (rds-djh1002-hodson-rds) and RFS-NFS (rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs) space. However, in the self-service portal he is still showing as “Provisioning”.
I just wanted to check whether there is anything we can do to speed up access to the data, or whether there is something we may have missed on our end.
Many thanks for your help.
Best wishes, 
Joanna
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS & RFS / Provisioning
Technical Action Required: No (user action pending)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (access pending)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
A new user (vj292) has been added to both an RDS and RFS-NFS project via the self-service portal but remains in “Provisioning” state. The most common cause of this status is that the user has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions in the UIS Self Service portal. Until this step is completed, the provisioning workflow cannot proceed and access will not be activated.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm provisioning state (internal)
   - Verify that the user appears as “Provisioning” on the relevant RDS and RFS projects.
   - Check whether Terms & Conditions acceptance is outstanding.

2) Instruct user to accept Terms & Conditions
   Ask the new member (vj292) to:
   - Visit: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/
   - Click on each relevant storage project:
       • rds-djh1002-hodson-rds
       • rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs
   - Accept the storage Terms & Conditions for each project.

3) Explain workflow
   - Once Terms & Conditions are accepted, a provisioning request is automatically generated.
   - HPC Support will then activate access.
   - All relevant parties will receive automatic email confirmation once provisioning completes.

4) Do NOT:
   - Manually override provisioning before Terms & Conditions are accepted.
   - Escalate unless provisioning remains stalled after acceptance.

IF PROVISIONING DOES NOT COMPLETE AFTER ACCEPTANCE

- Confirm acceptance timestamp in portal.
- Check whether an automatic provisioning ticket was generated.
- Escalate to storage administration only if backend provisioning fails after acceptance.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Provisioning status for new lab member

Dear Joanna,

Thank you for your message.

Victor’s access is currently showing as “Provisioning” because he has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions.

Could he please visit:

  https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/

and click on the relevant project links to accept the Terms & Conditions for each storage project?

Once this is completed, a provisioning request will automatically be generated and we will activate his access. All parties will receive confirmation by email once this has been processed.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not bypass the mandatory Terms & Conditions acceptance process.
- Do not grant access before provisioning workflow completes.
- Ensure both RDS and RFS projects are covered if both were requested.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management [3]
Technical Action Required: Yes [3]
Portal Action Required: Yes [2]
Urgency: Medium [3]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has added Victor to their RDS and RFS-NFS space, but Victor's status is still showing as "Provisioning" [3]. This suggests that Victor may not have accepted the terms and conditions for using the RDS storage [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Please ask Victor to log in to the self-service portal at https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/ and accept the terms and conditions for using the RDS storage [3]. If Victor has already accepted the terms and conditions, please ask him to log out and log back in to ensure that his session has picked up the new group membership [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Has Victor accepted the terms and conditions for using the RDS storage?
- Has Victor logged out and logged back in after being added to the RDS space?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Please ask Victor to log in to the self-service portal at https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/ and accept the terms and conditions for using the RDS storage. If he has already done so, please ask him to log out and log back in to ensure that his session has picked up the new group membership.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users must accept the terms and conditions for using RDS storage before they can access it [3].
- Users may need to log out and log back in after being added to an RDS space to ensure that their session has picked up the new group membership [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html::chunk::0004
[3] : HPCSSUP-88461

MFA please see our user documentation page:https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html)

You will now have access to the portal.

**Accepting the Invitation**

Upon entering the system, a pop-up notification will prompt you to accept the invitation. To continue, simply click on “Accept Invitation.”

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)

**Once accepted, users need to wait approximately 5 seconds for synchronization. A pop-up notification will appear at the top right of the screen confirming that the invitation has been accepted.**

Upon acceptance, you will be directed to the home page. The next step involves reading and agreeing to the terms and conditions.

Dawn has several policies that must be accepted before you can utilize any of its services. Once you log in, a list of these policies will be presented for your acceptance. You will encounter them on screens that resemble the following format.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/accept_terms.png)](../_images/accept_terms.png)

Upon accepting the policy, you will be online and ready to begin using the system.

## PI Managing Projects[¶](#pi-managing-projects "Permalink to this headline")

**Understanding roles and permissions for Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal:**

There are three roles you can have on a project:

* Principal Investigator
  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Co-Investigator
  :   + Can invite team

Please provide the email address of the individual you wish to invite, and select their corresponding role.

At the bottom of the page, you will find a support option. Please click on it and copy the email address: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk .

[![Keycloak_signin_support](../_images/Keycloak_signin_support.png)](../_images/Keycloak_signin_support.png)

When composing your email, make sure to include the title “Identity of last resort on Cambridge Access Portal” in the subject line. This will help the support team to identify your request promptly.

Please provide the name of your organisation along with the email address that will be used to access the Portal.

[![Support_email_draft](../_images/Support_email_draft.png)](../_images/Support_email_draft.png)

Muhammad Ahmed

benchmark_annotation.summary: Getting write permission to rds project

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS Permissions / Project Roles
Technical Action Required: No (permission governance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (write access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requests read/write access to an RDS project and assumes only one Data Manager can exist. On RDS projects, multiple Data Managers are allowed. If the user is currently a Data User, write capability should be granted by project governance (Data Owner/Data Manager) either by role reassignment or by applying permissions according to RDS permissions guidance.

ACTION

1) Clarify role model
   - Data Owners can appoint multiple Data Managers.
   - Data Users cannot self-assign elevated permissions.

2) Direct user to project governance path
   - Ask user to contact the Data Owner or existing Data Manager for:
     a) role change to Data Manager, or
     b) permission grant aligned with RDS ACL guidance.

3) Provide documentation link
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rds/permissions.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Emilia,

Data Owners can add as many Data Managers as required on an RDS project.

If you are currently a Data User, you will need to ask the Data Owner or a Data Manager to either:
1) reassign your role to Data Manager, or  
2) grant the required permissions in line with the RDS permissions guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rds/permissions.html

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not change project roles/permissions without Data Owner or Data Manager authorisation.
- Do not imply Helpdesk can bypass project governance for access control.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management [5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [5]
Escalation Required: Possibly [5]
Urgency: Medium [5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting read and write access to the RDS project rds-bru20-ukbb with project ID isCN5CtgAac [5]. By default, users have read-only access, and data managers need to create a subdirectory for users to write to [5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the data manager of the project needs to create a subdirectory for the user to write to [5]. Alternatively, the user's permissions can be updated to include write access [7].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS CURRENT STATE
- Are you currently listed as a data manager or user for the project rds-bru20-ukbb [5]?
- Can you confirm the current permissions for your user ID on the project [7]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Emilia,
To gain write access to the RDS project rds-bru20-ukbb, the data manager of the project needs to create a subdirectory for you to write to [5]. Alternatively, we can update your permissions to include write access [7]. Please let us know how you would like to proceed.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Data managers are responsible for managing user permissions and creating subdirectories for users to write to [5].
- Users should not have write access to the root directory of an RDS project [5].

REFERENCE KEY
[5] : HPCSSUP-34408
[7] : HPCSSUP-54197

**Understanding roles and permissions for Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal:**

There are three roles you can have on a project:

* Co-Investigator
  :   + Can invite team

The Principal Investigator (PI) is a special role that should be held only by the person (or people) who are legally responsible for the project.

In doing so, the PI confirms that they have conducted the required due diligence on the person they are inviting, and that they are happy to take responsibility for the actions of the person they invite.

benchmark_annotation.summary: HPC Job Scheduling Error

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Scheduling / Service Level Priority
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (long wait time but system functioning)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports a GPU job taking almost two weeks to start and asks whether there is an issue with their setup.

From the job script:
- Account: SVOBODA-SL3-GPU
- Partition: ampere
- GPUs requested: 4
- Wall time: 12:00:00

There are no configuration errors visible in the submission script. The most likely cause of delay is scheduler priority and service level.

Key scheduling principles:
- SL3 accounts operate at the non-paying service level.
- Paying service-level jobs receive higher priority.
- Priority increases over time while waiting (aging factor).
- Large GPU requests (e.g. 4 GPUs) may experience longer queue times depending on demand.
- Cancelling and resubmitting resets priority and can increase overall wait time.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm no configuration errors
   - The script contains valid SBATCH directives.
   - Correct partition and account syntax used.
   - No obvious resource mis-specification.

2) Explain scheduling behaviour
   - Jobs are prioritised according to service level and fair-share policy.
   - Paying activity can temporarily delay SL3 jobs.
   - Jobs steadily gain priority the longer they wait.

3) Provide best practice guidance
   - Do not cancel and resubmit while waiting.
   - Consider reducing resource request (e.g., fewer GPUs) if flexible.
   - Use `squeue -u <CRSid>` to monitor queue position.
   - Use `scontrol show job <jobid>` to inspect priority factors if desired.

4) Do NOT:
   - Escalate unless there is evidence of scheduler malfunction.
   - Promise faster scheduling.
   - Modify account priority.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the Job ID?
- Has the job been pending (PD) the entire time?
- What does `scontrol show job <jobid>` report under Reason= ?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC Job Scheduling Delay

Dear Amogh,

From the information provided, there is no obvious sign of a problem with your account or submission script. Your job is running under an SL3 (non-paying) GPU account, and at times there can be significant demand from higher-priority paying accounts.

Please note that each paying job can occupy its allocated GPUs for up to 36 hours. Jobs steadily increase in priority the longer they wait, so cancelling and resubmitting would be counterproductive as it resets that priority.

Your job will run when sufficient resources become available.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply scheduler malfunction without evidence.
- Do not recommend priority manipulation.
- Avoid suggesting users circumvent service-level policy.
- Reinforce correct queueing behaviour and expectations.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / Job Submission [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user's job took almost 2 weeks to get scheduled on the HPC, and they are wondering if there were any issues with their setup [1]. The job script provided requests 4 GPUs and has a time limit of 12 hours [1]. According to the documentation, the per-job, per-user GPU limits are currently 64 and 32 GPUs for SL1/2 and SL3 respectively [1]. The user's job is within these limits, but the waiting time is unusually long [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check the job's status in the queue using `squeue` [6].
2. Verify that the user's account has sufficient credits and is not blocked [2].
3. Review the job script for any errors or inconsistencies [1].
4. Consider resubmitting the job with a lower number of GPUs or a shorter time limit to reduce waiting time [5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about the job's output and error files?
- Have you tried submitting the job to a different partition, such as `icelake` or `icelake-himem` [6]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your job submission issue. We have reviewed your job script and account information, and we are investigating the cause of the delay. In the meantime, we recommend trying to resubmit the job with a lower number of GPUs or a shorter time limit to reduce waiting time. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node [1].
- The user's job is within the per-job, per-user GPU limits, but the waiting time is unusually long [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/a100.html::chunk::0001
[2] : HPCSSUP-70211
[5] : HPCSSUP-87714
[6] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/quickstart.html::chunk::0008
metrics.user_input:
To whom it may concern,
I have submitted a job on the HPC, and it took almost 2 weeks to get scheduled. I was wondering if there were any issues with my setup. The job script I used is below.
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J job_gpu_1
#SBATCH -o /home/av670/logfiles/job_gpu_1.log
#SBATCH -A SVOBODA-SL3-GPU
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
echo "This job is running on: $(hostname)"
echo "Present working directory: $(pwd)"
echo "Job ID: $SLURM_JOB_ID"
echo "Job start time: $(date)"
module purge
echo "loading python"
module load python/3.11.0-icl
echo "loading cuda"
module load cuda/12.1
echo "activating venv"
source /rds/user/av670/hpc-work/venvs/venv/bin/activate
echo "running file"
python -u /home/av670/projects/part-ii-project/job.py
Kind regards,
Amogh Vishwakarma

retrieval_trace.text:
please see [Jobs requiring MPI](#jobs-requiring-mpi)
  for more information.

## Recommendations for running on ampere[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

Since the gpu-q nodes are running Rocky8, you will want to recompile
your applications. We suggest you do this by requesting an
[interactive node](#software).

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

The per-job, per-user GPU limits are currently 64 and 32 GPUs for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for ampere[¶](#default-submission-script-for-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Ampere default job submission script modified for the ampere nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs, but can be modified for other types
of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts, please see
the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p ampere
```

Slurm will enforce allocation of a proportional number of CPUs (32) per GPU.

Note that if you either do not specify a number of GPUs per node with *–gres*, or request more than one node
with less than 4 GPUs per node, you will receive an error on

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Weird queueing time for a ampere GPU job

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear System Admin,
I'm writing to enquire about some suspicious behaviour of a GPU job that I submitted on Wilkes. The id of the job is 51213406, and I was requesting 12 GPU hours for one single A100 GP. This job has been queueing for 5 days and 4 hours and has never started to run until I killed it just now. This behaviour seems quite suspicious, so I'm writing to see if any potential causes could be traced.

node from the range 1 to 4, which is done via the directive

where 1 <= N <= 4.

Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA Ampere A100 GPUs, with 1000GB host RAM and two AMD EPYC 64-core processors.

Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 3 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.

### Submitting the job to the queuing system[¶](#submitting-the-job-to-the-queuing-system "Permalink to this headline")

The command sbatch is used to submit jobs, e.g.

```
sbatch submission_script
```

The command will return a unique job identifier, which is used to query and control the job and to identify output. See the man page (*man sbatch*) for more options.

The following more complex example submits a job array with index values between 1 and 7 with a step size of 2 (i.e. 1, 3, 5 and 7) to the project STARS-SL2-CPU:

```
sbatch --array=1-7:2 -A STARS-SL2-CPU submission_script
```

### Deleting jobs[¶](#deleting-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

To cancel a job (either running or still queuing) use scancel:

```
scancel <jobid>
```

The <jobid> is printed when the job is submitted, alternatively use the commands squeue, qstat or showq to obtain the job ID.

retrieved_contexts:
please see [Jobs requiring MPI](#jobs-requiring-mpi)
  for more information.

## Recommendations for running on ampere[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

Since the gpu-q nodes are running Rocky8, you will want to recompile
your applications. We suggest you do this by requesting an
[interactive node](#software).

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

The per-job, per-user GPU limits are currently 64 and 32 GPUs for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for ampere[¶](#default-submission-script-for-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Ampere default job submission script modified for the ampere nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs, but can be modified for other types
of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts, please see
the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p ampere
```

Slurm will enforce allocation of a proportional number of CPUs (32) per GPU.

Note that if you either do not specify a number of GPUs per node with *–gres*, or request more than one node
with less than 4 GPUs per node, you will receive an error on

benchmark_annotation.summary: Issues with GPU compilation of a package

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Compilation / GPU (Ampere, ukaea-amp)
Technical Action Required: No (user rebuild with supported stack)
Escalation Required: No (unless failure persists after rebuild)
Urgency: Medium (runtime failure on GPU partition)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User successfully compiled LAMMPS + symmetrix with a custom CUDA 12.4.1 toolchain, but runtime fails on ukaea-amp with:

- Open MPI component warnings (libosmcomp.so.4, libfabric.so.1 not found)
- Process exited on signal 4 (Illegal instruction)

Likely causes:
1) Incompatible Open MPI module (built for Skylake + RHEL7) being used on Ampere (Zen3 + RHEL8).
2) Instruction-set mismatch (e.g. -march=native during cross-compilation on login node).
3) Mixed CUDA/MPI/compiler stack leading to ABI/runtime incompatibilities.

Resolution is to rebuild using the supported Ampere test software stack with a consistent compiler/CUDA/MPI toolchain and disable native CPU flags when cross-compiling.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Ensure clean environment
   - Start a fresh shell.
   - Remove old build directory:
       cd /path/to/lammps
       rm -rf build-gpu

2) Load base modules
       module purge
       module load rhel8/global rhel8/slurm

3) Enable Ampere test stack
       module use /usr/local/software/spack/csd3/spack-modules/test/ampere-2025-06-01/linux-rocky8-zen3

4) Load compatible toolchain
       module load gcc/14.3.0/vlhhcp6m \
                   cuda/12.8.1/gcc/kdeps6ab \
                   openmpi/4.1.8/gcc/hemliivg \
                   cmake/3.31.10/gcc/7ddsybx7

   Rationale:
   - OpenMPI built for Zen3 and RHEL8
   - CUDA ≥12 satisfied
   - Consistent compiler/runtime stack

5) Reconfigure build
       cmake \
           -B build-gpu \
           -D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD=20 \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED=ON \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=$(pwd)/lib/kokkos/bin/nvcc_wrapper \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -march=znver3 -ffast-math" \
           -D BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
           -D BUILD_OMP=ON \
           -D BUILD_MPI=ON \
           -D PKG_KOKKOS=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_SERIAL=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_OPENMP=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ARCH_ZEN3=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_AGGRESSIVE_VECTORIZATION=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_CUDA=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ARCH_AMPERE80=ON \
           -D SYMMETRIX_KOKKOS=ON \
           -D SYMMETRIX_SPHERICART_CUDA=ON \
           -D SPHERICART_ARCH_NATIVE=OFF \
           cmake

   Important:
   - Disable -march=native (SPHERICART_ARCH_NATIVE=OFF) when building on login node.
   - Ensure CMake does not mix multiple CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER definitions.

6) Build
       cmake --build build-gpu -j

7) Runtime
   - Load the same modules used for compilation before executing.
   - Submit via Slurm on ukaea-amp.
   - Do not mix MPI modules between build and run.

EXPECTED OUTCOME
- No illegal instruction at runtime.
- No Open MPI component mismatch warnings.
- GPU-enabled LAMMPS executable runs on ukaea-amp partition.

IF ISSUE PERSISTS
Request:
- Full submission script
- Full build log
- Exact runtime command
- Output of:
      module list
      ldd <lammps_binary>
      ompi_info | head

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Issues with GPU compilation of a package

Hello,

The runtime failure is most likely due to a mixed or incompatible CUDA/MPI/compiler stack rather than the package source itself. Please rebuild the code using a clean environment and the current Ampere test software stack, making sure that the same modules are used consistently for both build and run.

In particular, please avoid carrying over older OpenMPI or compiler modules, and disable native CPU-targeting flags when configuring the build on a login node. If the problem persists after rebuilding, please send the full build log, your submission script, the exact runtime command, and the outputs of `module list`, `ldd <lammps_binary>`, and `ompi_info | head`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not recommend installing custom system-level CUDA drivers.
- Avoid mixing MPI modules from different OS/compiler stacks.
- Ensure consistent module environment between compilation and runtime.
- Do not suggest modifying system libraries under /usr.

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Compilation / GPU (Ampere, ukaea-amp)
Technical Action Required: No (user rebuild with supported stack)
Escalation Required: No (unless failure persists after rebuild)
Urgency: Medium (runtime failure on GPU partition)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User successfully compiled LAMMPS + symmetrix with a custom CUDA 12.4.1 toolchain, but runtime fails on ukaea-amp with:

- Open MPI component warnings (libosmcomp.so.4, libfabric.so.1 not found)
- Process exited on signal 4 (Illegal instruction)

Likely causes:
1) Incompatible Open MPI module (built for Skylake + RHEL7) being used on Ampere (Zen3 + RHEL8).
2) Instruction-set mismatch (e.g. -march=native during cross-compilation on login node).
3) Mixed CUDA/MPI/compiler stack leading to ABI/runtime incompatibilities.

Resolution is to rebuild using the supported Ampere test software stack with a consistent compiler/CUDA/MPI toolchain and disable native CPU flags when cross-compiling.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Ensure clean environment
   - Start a fresh shell.
   - Remove old build directory:
       cd /path/to/lammps
       rm -rf build-gpu

2) Load base modules
       module purge
       module load rhel8/global rhel8/slurm

3) Enable Ampere test stack
       module use /usr/local/software/spack/csd3/spack-modules/test/ampere-2025-06-01/linux-rocky8-zen3

4) Load compatible toolchain
       module load gcc/14.3.0/vlhhcp6m \
                   cuda/12.8.1/gcc/kdeps6ab \
                   openmpi/4.1.8/gcc/hemliivg \
                   cmake/3.31.10/gcc/7ddsybx7

   Rationale:
   - OpenMPI built for Zen3 and RHEL8
   - CUDA ≥12 satisfied
   - Consistent compiler/runtime stack

5) Reconfigure build
       cmake \
           -B build-gpu \
           -D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD=20 \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED=ON \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=$(pwd)/lib/kokkos/bin/nvcc_wrapper \
           -D CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -march=znver3 -ffast-math" \
           -D BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
           -D BUILD_OMP=ON \
           -D BUILD_MPI=ON \
           -D PKG_KOKKOS=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_SERIAL=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_OPENMP=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ARCH_ZEN3=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_AGGRESSIVE_VECTORIZATION=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ENABLE_CUDA=ON \
           -D Kokkos_ARCH_AMPERE80=ON \
           -D SYMMETRIX_KOKKOS=ON \
           -D SYMMETRIX_SPHERICART_CUDA=ON \
           -D SPHERICART_ARCH_NATIVE=OFF \
           cmake

   Important:
   - Disable -march=native (SPHERICART_ARCH_NATIVE=OFF) when building on login node.
   - Ensure CMake does not mix multiple CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER definitions.

6) Build
       cmake --build build-gpu -j

7) Runtime
   - Load the same modules used for compilation before executing.
   - Submit via Slurm on ukaea-amp.
   - Do not mix MPI modules between build and run.

EXPECTED OUTCOME
- No illegal instruction at runtime.
- No Open MPI component mismatch warnings.
- GPU-enabled LAMMPS executable runs on ukaea-amp partition.

IF ISSUE PERSISTS
Request:
- Full submission script
- Full build log
- Exact runtime command
- Output of:
      module list
      ldd <lammps_binary>
      ompi_info | head

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Issues with GPU compilation of a package

Hello,

The runtime failure is most likely due to a mixed or incompatible CUDA/MPI/compiler stack rather than the package source itself. Please rebuild the code using a clean environment and the current Ampere test software stack, making sure that the same modules are used consistently for both build and run.

In particular, please avoid carrying over older OpenMPI or compiler modules, and disable native CPU-targeting flags when configuring the build on a login node. If the problem persists after rebuilding, please send the full build log, your submission script, the exact runtime command, and the outputs of `module list`, `ldd <lammps_binary>`, and `ompi_info | head`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not recommend installing custom system-level CUDA drivers.
- Avoid mixing MPI modules from different OS/compiler stacks.
- Ensure consistent module environment between compilation and runtime.
- Do not suggest modifying system libraries under /usr.

gcc/9.4.0/gcc-11.2.0-72sgv5z
# download LAMMPS
git clone --depth=1 --branch stable_29Aug2024_update2 https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git lammps_gpu

cd lammps_gpu
# create and switch to build directory
mkdir build && cd build

# setup with gcc compiler preset.
cmake -C ../cmake/presets/gcc.cmake ../cmake
# activate support for EAM forcefields
cmake -D PKG_MANYBODY=yes .
# activate support for Ampere GPUs
cmake -D PKG_GPU=yes -D GPU_ARCH=sm_80 -D GPU_API=cuda .
# activate colloid package (used in sbatch example). Replace with any package you plan to use
cmake -D PKG_COLLOID=yes .

# compile lammps
cmake --build . --clean-first

cd ../..
```

This will also produce an executable `lmp` in the `build` directory which you can use with `eam` pair style.

## Running LAMMPS[¶](#running-lammps "Permalink to this headline")

LAMMPS can be run with a sbatch script similar to:

```
#!/bin/bash
#! update the Account and partition (-p) in the two following lines to suit your needs.
#SBATCH -A MYACCOUNT
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -t 8:00:00
#SBATCH --exclusive
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --tasks-per-node=8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH -o lammps.out
#SBATCH -e lammps.err

module purge
# Use default module relevant to the partition you set in the SBATCH directives.
module load rhel8/default-amp

app="lammps_gpu/build/lmp" # Assuming lammps was installed on this directory, see setup. Edit otherwise.
infile="lammps_gpu/examples/colloid/in.colloid"

# Creating MPS wrapper script to run multiple MPI jobs per GPU
rm -f mps-wrapper.sh

cat <<EOF >> mps-wrapper.sh
#!/bin/bash
# Example

Intel compilers preset.
cmake -C ../cmake/presets/intel.cmake ../cmake
# activate the reaxff package

This will produce an executable `lmp` in that directory which you can then use with the `pair/reax` type.

Some LAMMPS subpackages support optimised versions for the specific hardware on CSD3. For example the EAM force-field has support for CPU and GPU specific optimisations. To build a configuration that supports running EAM calculations on CPU :

```
# load the CSD3 modules for CPU, INTEL and CMAKE
module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/intel
module load intel-oneapi-mkl/2024.1.0/intel/vnktbkgm
module load cmake/3.29.4/gcc/cery5wyj
# download LAMMPS
git clone --branch stable_29Aug2024_update2 --depth=1 https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git
cd lammps

# create and switch to build directory
mkdir build && cd build

# setup with Intel compilers preset.
cmake -C ../cmake/presets/intel.cmake ../cmake
# activate support for EAM forcefields
cmake -D PKG_MANYBODY=yes .
# activate intel-optimised support for intel architectures
cmake -D PKG_INTEL=yes -D INTEL_ARCH=cpu -D INTEL_LRT_MODE=c++11 .

# compile lammps
cmake --build . --clean-first
cd ../..
```

This will produce the executable `lmp` for Intel CPU architecture. To build a configuration that supports running EAM calculations on the Ampere partition:

```
# load the CSD3 modules for the GPU architecture

module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load cmake/3.21.4/gcc-9.4.0-pucmh2y
module load

# OpenMM[¶](#openmm "Permalink to this headline")

From the OpenMM website <http://openmm.org/>

> OpenMM is a high performance toolkit for molecular simulation. Use it as a library, or as an application. We include extensive language bindings for Python, C, C++, and even Fortran. The code is open source and actively maintained on Github, licensed under MIT and LGPL. Part of the Omnia suite of tools for predictive biomolecular simulation.

## Installing OpenMM on CSD3[¶](#installing-openmm-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Below are instructions of how to install openmm in a miniconda virtual environment. Part of the installation instructions are located at <http://docs.openmm.org/latest/userguide/application.html#installing-openmm> .

Connect to a login node with:

```
ssh your-username@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
# Install miniconda 3 (skip the following 4 lines if you already have it)
mkdir -p miniconda3
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -O miniconda3/miniconda.sh
bash miniconda3/miniconda.sh -b -u -p miniconda3
rm miniconda3/miniconda.sh

# Install and test openmm. * This operation must be done on a GPU node (interactive session) for the tests to be successful *
# Check for cuda version
if [ -z "$CUDA_PATH" ]; then
    module load cuda
fi
if [ -z "$CUDA_PATH" ]; then
    echo "Module load failed, aborting"
    return 1
fi
CUDA_VERSION=$(basename $CUDA_PATH)
if [ -z "$CUDA_VERSION" ]; then
    echo "Error, could not extract CUDA version, aborting"
fi
# module load cuda if

module load gcc-5.4.0-gcc-4.8.5-fis24gg

### GCC Compiler Suite[¶](#gcc-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The GCC compiler suite is accessed by loading the `gcc` module. For example:

```
module load gcc-7.2.0-gcc-4.8.5-pqn7o2k
```

Once you have loaded the module, the compilers are available as:

* `gfortran` - Fortran
* `gcc` - C
* `g++` - C++

### PGI Compiler Suite[¶](#pgi-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The Portland Group (PGI) compilers are available under the `pgi` modules. For example:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

Once you have loaded the module, the compilers are available as:

* `pgfortran` - Fortran
* `pgcc` - C
* `pg++` - C++

## Compiling MPI codes[¶](#compiling-mpi-codes "Permalink to this headline")

There are two prefered MPI libraries currently available on CSD3:

* Intel MPI
* OpenMPI

### Using Intel MPI[¶](#using-intel-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

To compile MPI code with Intel MPI, using any compiler, you must first load the
`intel/bundles/complib/2020.2` module (which on the cclake CPU nodes is
loaded by default as part of the `rhel7/default-ccl` module):

```
module load intel/bundles/complib/2020.2
```

This makes the compiler wrapper scripts available to you. To change the
